muharram.description: **Muharram**[a] ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): ٱلْمُحَرَّم, romanized: al-Muḥarram) is the first month of the [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar). It is one of the four sacred months of the year when warfare is banned. It precedes the month of [Safar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Safar). The tenth of Muharram is known as [Ashura](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ashura), an important day of commemoration in [Islam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islam). For [Sunni Muslims](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunni_Islam), the day commemorates the [parting of the Red Sea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crossing_the_Red_Sea) by [Moses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moses_in_Islam) and the salvation of the [Israelites](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Israelites), observed through voluntary [fasting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fasting) and other permissible expressions of gratitude. By contrast, Ashura is a day of mourning for [Shia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shia_Islam) Muslims, who annually commemorate the death of [Husayn ibn Ali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Husayn_ibn_Ali), grandson of the [Islamic prophet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prophets_and_messengers_in_Islam) [Muhammad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muhammad) and the third [Shia imam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Imamate_in_Shia_doctrine). Husayn was killed, alongside most of his relatives and his small retinue, in the [Battle of Karbala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Karbala) in 680 [CE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Common_Era) against the army of the [Umayyad caliph](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate) [Yazid ibn Mu'awiya](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yazid_I) (r. 680–683). The Shia rituals span the first ten days of Muharram, culminating on Ashura with mourning processions in Shia cities. Also in Muharram, the [Aqsa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Al-Aqsa_Mosque) mosque in [Jerusalem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jerusalem) was initially set as the direction of prayer for early Muslims.

events.title: Death of Caliph Umar
details: 1 Muharram — Death of Caliph Umar (r. 634–644) by injuries from the attack of Persian slave Abu Lu'lu'a Firuz. Sunnis carry rallies on 1 Muharram to commemorate Umar.

events.title: Most mourning rituals for Karbala take place during the first ten days of Muharram, culminating on the tenth with processions in major Shia cities
details: 2–10 Muharram — Most mourning rituals for Karbala take place during the first ten days of Muharram, culminating on the tenth with processions in major Shia cities.

events.title: Baba Farid, a Punjabi Sufi saint, died on this day in 1266
details: 5 Muharram — Baba Farid, a Punjabi Sufi saint, died on this day in 1266. His death is celebrated (urs) for six days during Muharram, in Pakpattan, Pakistan.

events.title: In what became known as the 1782 Muharram Rebellion, on this day Bengali Muslims in Sylhet staged one of the earliest anti-British uprisings in the Indian subcontinent
details: 8 Muharram — In what became known as the 1782 Muharram Rebellion, on this day Bengali Muslims in Sylhet staged one of the earliest anti-British uprisings in the Indian subcontinent.

events.title: On this day, the Islamic prophet Muhammad set the Aqsa mosque in Jerusalem as the qibla, towards which early Muslims prayed
details: 16 Muharram — On this day, the Islamic prophet Muhammad set the Aqsa mosque in Jerusalem as the qibla, towards which early Muslims prayed. This was superseded later by the ancient Ka'ba sanctuary in Mecca in connection with verse 2:144 of the Quran, the central religious text in Islam.

events.title: Arrival of the "people of the elephant" in Mecca, a reference to al-Fil
details: 17 Muharram — Arrival of the "people of the elephant" in Mecca, a reference to al-Fil (lit. 'the elephant'), a surah (chapter) in the Quran.

safar.description:
**Safar** ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): صَفَر, romanized: Ṣafar), also spelt as **Safer** in [Turkish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turkish_language),[1] is the second month of the lunar [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar).

Most of the Islamic months were named according to ancient Sabean/Sabaic weather conditions; however, since the calendar is lunar, the months shift by about 11 days every solar year, meaning that these conditions do not necessarily correspond to the name of the month.

events.title: Arba'een or Chehlum

rabi-al-awwal.description: **Rabi I** ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): ‏رَبِيع الْأَوَّل‎, romanized: Rabīʿ al-ʾAwwal,[a] lit. 'Rabi the First')[b] is the third month of the [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar). The name *Rabīʿ al-ʾAwwal* means 'the first [spring](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spring_(season))', referring to its position in the [pre-Islamic Arabian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pre-Islamic_Arabia) calendar.

rabi-ath-thani.description: **Rabi II** ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): ‏رَبِيع الثَّانِي‎, romanized: Rabīʿ ath-Thānī,[a] lit. 'Rabi the Second', more commonly called ‏رَبِيع الْآخِر‎ Rabīʿ al-ʾĀkhir,[b] lit. 'Rabi the Last')[c] is the fourth month of the [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar). The name *Rabīʿ ath-Thānī* means "the second spring" in [Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic), referring to its position in the [pre-Islamic Arabian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pre-Islamic_Arabia) calendar.

jumada-I.description:
**Jumada I** ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): ‏جُمَادَى ٱلْأُولَى‎, romanized: Jumādā l-ʾŪlā,[a] lit. 'Jumada the First') is the fifth month of the [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar). Jumada I spans 29 or 30 days. The origin of the month's name is theorized by some as coming from the word *jamād* ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): جماد), meaning "arid, dry, or cold",[1] denoting the dry and parched land and hence the dry months of the [pre-Islamic Arabian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pre-Islamic_Arabia) calendar. *Jumādā* ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): جُمَادَى) may also be related to a verb meaning "to freeze", and another account relates that water would freeze during this time of year. The secondary name *Jumādā l-ʾŪlā* may possibly mean "to take charge with, commend, entrust, commit or care during the arid or cold month".[1] However, this explanation is rejected by some as *Jumādā al-ʾŪlā* is a lunar month that does not coincide with seasons as solar months do.

In Turkish, the name of the month is *cemaziyelevvel* (from [Ottoman Turkish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ottoman_Turkish_language): جمادی الاول, romanized: cemaziyü'l-evvel);[2][3] it was abbreviated as جا *ca* in [Ottoman Turkish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ottoman_Turkish).[2][4]

events.title: the Battle of Mu'ta took place in the village of the same name
details: On 1 Jumada I 8 AH (September 629) — the Battle of Mu'ta took place in the village of the same name (in present-day Jordan), in which the Muslim army (dispatched by Muhammad) fought against the army of the Byzantine Empire and their Ghassanid vassals reportedly in retribution for the murder of a Muslim emissary by a Ghassanid chief, Shurahbil ibn Amr. The battle was won by the Byzantines, and amongst the losses the Muslims incurred were three of the most prominent Companions of Muhammad): Zayd ibn Haritha (his adopted son, known as the fourth person to become a Muslim); Ja‘far ibn Abi Talib (his cousin), and ‘Abd Allah ibn Rawaha (present at the second pledge at al-‘Aqaba), whom Muhammad had specifically appointed to be generals of the Muslim army in the battle, so that if one of them should be killed, the other would take his place.

jumada-II.description: **Jumada II** ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): ‏جُمَادَى ٱلثَّانِيَة‎, romanized: Jumādā th-Thāniya,[a] lit. 'Jumada the Second', more commonly called ‏جُمَادَى ٱلْآخِرَة‎, Jumādā l-ʾĀkhira,[b] lit. 'Jumada the Last')[c] is the sixth month of the [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar). The word *jamād* (‏جماد‎), from which the name of the month is derived, is used to denote dry, parched land, a land devoid of rain. *Jumādā* (جمادى) may also be related to a verb meaning 'to freeze', and another account relates that water would freeze in [pre-Islamic Arabia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pre-Islamic_Arabia) during this time of year.

rajab.description:
**Rajab** ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): رَجَب) is the seventh month of the [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar). The lexical definition of the [classical Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classical_Arabic) verb *rajaba* is "to respect",[1] which could also mean "be awe or be in fear", of which Rajab is a derivative.

This month is regarded as one of the four sacred months (including [Muharram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muharram), [Dhu'l-Qa'da](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dhu'l-Qa'da) and [Dhu'l-Hijja](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dhu'l-Hijja)) in Islam in which battles are prohibited. The [pre-Islamic Arabs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pre-Islamic_Arabia) also considered warfare to be blasphemous during these four months.[1]

Muslims believe Rajab is the month in which [‘Alī ibn Abī Tālib](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/‘Alī_ibn_Abī_Tālib), the first Shia Imam and the fourth [Rashidun caliph](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rashidun_caliph), was born.

details: The Battle of Tabouk took place in Rajab, 9 AH (October 630).

details: 7 Rajab — Twelvers observe the Festival of Imam Musa al-Kazim in dedication of Musā' al-Kādhim. This is so as to avoid missing celebrating the birth of the seventh imam, which took place in Safar.

details: 22 Rajab — In India and Pakistan, Koonday (tablecloth dinner) is organized. It is an occasion for Muslims to discuss Allah and the Ahlul Bayt and to strengthen ties among the Sunni Sufi community with love and compassion.

shaban.description:
**Shaʽban** ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): شَعْبَان *Šaʿbān*) is the eighth month of the [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar). It is called the month of 'separation', as the word means 'to disperse' or 'to separate' because the [pre-Islamic Arabs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Religion_in_pre-Islamic_Arabia) used to disperse in search of water.[1]

The fifteenth night of this month is [Mid-Sha'ban](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mid-Sha'ban),[2] which coincides with the celebration of [Shab-e-Barat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shab-e-Barat) in Muslim communities all over Asia.

Sha'ban is the last [lunar month](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lunar_month) before [Ramadan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramadan), and so Muslims determine in it when the first day of Ramadan fasting will be. In the second [Hijri year](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hijri_year) (624 CE), [fasting during Ramadan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fasting_during_Ramadan) was made obligatory during this month.[1]

ramadan.description:
**Ramadan**[a][b] is the ninth month of the [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar). It is observed by [Muslims](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muslim) worldwide as a month of [fasting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fasting) (*sawm*), communal [prayer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prayer) (*salah*), reflection, study of the Quran, charity, and strengthening community ties.[5] It is also the month in which the [Quran](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quran) is believed to have been revealed to the [Islamic prophet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prophets_of_Islam) [Muhammad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muhammad), known as [Laylat al-Qadr](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Night_of_Power). The annual observance of Ramadan is regarded as one of the [five pillars of Islam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Five_pillars_of_Islam)[6] and lasts 29 to 30 days, from one sighting of the [crescent moon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hilal_(crescent_moon)) to the next.[7][8]

Fasting from dawn to sunset is obligatory (*fard*) for all Muslims who have reached puberty,[9] and who are not [acutely](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acute_illness) or [chronically ill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chronic_illness), [traveling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Travel), [elderly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Old_age), [breastfeeding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Breastfeeding), [pregnant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnancy), or [menstruating](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Menstruation_in_Islam).[10] The predawn meal is *suhur*, and the nightly feast that breaks the fast is *iftar*.[11][12] Although rulings (*fatawa*) have been issued that Muslims who live in regions with a [midnight sun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Midnight_sun) or [polar night](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polar_night) should follow the timetable of [Mecca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mecca),[13] it is common practice to follow the timetable of the closest country in which night can be distinguished from day.[14][15][16]

The spiritual rewards (*thawab*) of fasting are believed to be multiplied during Ramadan.[17] Accordingly, during the hours of fasting, Muslims refrain not only from food and drink, but also from all behavior deemed to be [sinful in Islam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_views_on_sin).[18][19]

shawwal.description:
**Shawwal** ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): شَوَّال, romanized: Shawwāl) is the tenth month of the [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar). It comes after [Ramadan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramadan) and before [Dhu'l-Qa'da](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dhu'l-Qa'da).

*Shawwāl* stems from the Arabic verb *shāla* (شَالَ), which means to 'lift or carry',[1] generally to take or move things from one place to another.

events.title: Eid al-Fitr is celebrated throughout the Muslim World for the first three days of this month
details: Every 1st day of Shawwāl — Eid al-Fitr is celebrated throughout the Muslim World for the first three days of this month (but celebrations last until the 29th/30th day as per the festive season's duration).

dhul-qada.description:
**Dhu'l-Qa'da**[a] ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): ‏ذُو ٱلْقَعْدَة‎, *Ḏū l-Qaʿda*, IPA: [ðu‿l.qaʕ.da]) is the eleventh month in the [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar).

It could possibly mean "possessor or owner of the sitting and seating place" - the space occupied while sitting or the manner of the sitting, pose or posture.

It is one of the four [sacred months](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sacred_months) in Islam during which warfare is prohibited, hence the name "Master of [Truces](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Truce)".

dhul-hijja.description:
**Dhu'l-Hijja**[a] ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): ‏ذُو ٱلْحِجَّة‎, romanized: Ḏū l-Ḥijja IPA: [ðu‿l.ħid͡ʒːa]) is the twelfth and final month in the [Islamic calendar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_calendar).[1] Being one of the four [sacred months](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sacred_months) during which war is forbidden, it is the month in which the *Ḥajj* ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): حج, lit. 'pilgrimage') takes place as well as [Eid al-Adha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eid_al-Adha) ([Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language): عيد الأضحى, lit. 'the Festival of the Sacrifice').

The [Arabic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arabic_language) name of the month, *Dhu'l-Hijja*, means "Possessor of the Pilgrimage" or "The Month of the Pilgrimage".[1] During this month, Muslim pilgrims from all around the world congregate at [Mecca](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mecca) to visit the [Kaaba](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kaaba). The [Hajj rites](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hajj#Rites) begin on the eighth day and continue for four or five days. The [Day of Arafah](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Day_of_Arafah) takes place on the ninth of the month. [Eid al-Adha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eid_al-Adha), the "Festival of the Sacrifice", begins on the tenth day and ends on the thirteenth day.[1]

eventsIndex.title: On this day, the Islamic prophet Muhammad set the Aqsa mosque in Jerusalem as the qibla, towards which early Muslims prayed
details: 16 Muharram — On this day, the Islamic prophet Muhammad set the Aqsa mosque in Jerusalem as the qibla, towards which early Muslims prayed. This was superseded later by the ancient Ka'ba sanctuary in Mecca in connection with verse 2:144 of the Quran, the central religious text in Islam.

eventsIndex.title: the Battle of Mu'ta took place in the village of the same name
details: On 1 Jumada I 8 AH (September 629) — the Battle of Mu'ta took place in the village of the same name (in present-day Jordan), in which the Muslim army (dispatched by Muhammad) fought against the army of the Byzantine Empire and their Ghassanid vassals reportedly in retribution for the murder of a Muslim emissary by a Ghassanid chief, Shurahbil ibn Amr. The battle was won by the Byzantines, and amongst the losses the Muslims incurred were three of the most prominent Companions of Muhammad): Zayd ibn Haritha (his adopted son, known as the fourth person to become a Muslim); Ja‘far ibn Abi Talib (his cousin), and ‘Abd Allah ibn Rawaha (present at the second pledge at al-‘Aqaba), whom Muhammad had specifically appointed to be generals of the Muslim army in the battle, so that if one of them should be killed, the other would take his place.

eventsIndex.title: event of the mubahala took place
details: 24 Dhu'l-Hijja — event of the mubahala took place ('Eid al-Mubahilah).

eventsIndex.title: some historians mention that the Hadith, Ahl al-Kisa', event was also on the same day prior to Muhammad setting out for Mubahila
details: 24 Dhu'l-Hijja — some historians mention that the Hadith, Ahl al-Kisa', event was also on the same day prior to Muhammad setting out for Mubahila.

eventsIndex.title: supplication day and giving of alms with the ring by Ali
details: 24 Dhu'l-Hijja — supplication day and giving of alms with the ring by Ali. In reply verse, "Verily your Walee is Allah; and His Messenger and those who establish salah, and pay zakat while they be in Rukooʿ. (Maa-Idah: 55)" was revealed.

eventsIndex.title: Sura Al-Insan or Hal Ata, or Dahar, which records the giving of alms to orphans, the destitute and travellers by Fatimah Hasan and Husain was revealed
details: 25 Dhu'l-Hijja — Sura Al-Insan or Hal Ata, or Dahar, which records the giving of alms to orphans, the destitute and travellers by Fatimah Hasan and Husain was revealed.

eventsIndex.title: Ali becomes the Caliph of Islam – AH 35
details: 25 Dhu'l-Hijja — Ali becomes the Caliph of Islam – AH 35.[citation needed]
